story:
"42…43…44…45…" I count as I drive, leaving behind gunshots, blood, and the six pounds of pressure that ended the man who was both my business partner and my supplier—my husband. His last look lingers: eyes like dark wells, a smile that wasn't human. The house, the off-white rug turned red, the bourbon-stained cash swept from the oak table I chose back when I believed in his glittering world—all of it falls behind me. I aim for the one place I've marked on my way out: The Blue Magic.

I've been running since I was a kid, fetching errands for a mother who taught me that hell is other people and that names are clothes you change when they get dirty. This time I'm Summer Smith, courtesy of a favor in Devon County. The club waits like a neon chapel at the crossroads of sin and survival. Inside, the pole rises like an altar under magenta light; sweat, perfume, music, and money stitch together a congregation of people trying to turn pain into rent.

Then time bends. She appears—skin catching the light like polished onyx, movements measured like a prayer repeated past faith, past wanting, into survival. The room worships as bills rain down, but those autumn eyes find mine across the smoky room—the same ones that whispered escape plans to me three months ago in this very club. She's dancing to live, not to be adored, just as she was the night we first met and planned this moment. When the song ends and she steps off stage, our eyes lock with familiar, silent understanding.

"Give it up for Black Celeste!" the DJ cries, and the crowd swallows her from view. Then she's in front of me, all rhinestone and heat, voice soft and Southern; she reads the urgency in my face like a book we've both memorized. We trade a few words—the coded phrases we rehearsed months ago—before the night snaps. A drunk man grabs her. On instinct, my hand finds a Dom Pérignon bottle; glass meets bone, and security rushes toward the commotion as the room explodes into screams and breaking things. She laughs, grabs my hand, and we push through the chaos and run, the music thinning to a pulse behind us.

Against a chipped wall, our mouths crash. Breath and heartbeat drown the noise. She pulls back, eyes burning. "Are you sure he's gone? We can finally leave?" I tell her I put him down myself. Tonight was always the night—we planned it this way, chaos covering our tracks. We shove through the back door, ready to disappear into the night.

Click.

Five men in black—ski masks on, shotguns leveled. The emblem on their jackets is a ghost I thought I'd buried with him. Our car is gone. The alley is too quiet. A neat trap sprung by someone who always watched further than I did—maybe six months, maybe a year. I don't have time to figure out how long he knew. I just know he planned for this.

Her hand finds mine—steady, certain. Those autumn eyes promise we are not finished. I squeeze back. My heart pounds as the moment stretches. The bang slices the air. The world tilts, washed in white, everything collapsing into the space between this breath and whatever comes next.
